content: @Miguel Ojeda any chance you have had any further communication behind the scenes on https://github.com/rust-lang/rust-bindgen/issues/753? I see they added the Rust for Linux tag, but have still not responded. I'm potentially willing to just join whatever channels LLVM uses for communication and just do my best to implement the LLVM side of this if we just need someone to do the work. I don't know modern C++ well, but if the LLVM community is generally helpful, I can probably figure it out with some guidance.

I met with @Emilio Cobos Álvarez a few days ago, and we discussed this for a bit, including the bindgen-side solution/workaround of emitting extra code so that we can fetch the value.

The subvariations we discussed were "one pass per macro" (slow, but could work without user input), "one extra pass for all macros" (this would be fast, but would require the user to pass e.g. a regex of matching macros to evaluate that are "known to be constants" already) and "append the macros later on" (i.e. add them via a context or similar, which may be possible or not with the current API; we should also check e.g. what Cling does here).

So, in summary, bindgen would be happy with a workaround. But solving it in LLVM is, of course, the best long-term, so that would be great too.

For the LLVM side, Aaron Ballman (the Clang maintainer) told me he would be happy to review our changes if we sent them, in order to help bindgen, so that is great if someone wants to work on it.

(I will add this into our issue list)
I added it to the list (improving it a bit too) and Cc'd you there, so that people is aware you have been helping with this.
So, yeah, if you want to work on either side of it, in any of the variations, that is very welcome John.
@John Baublitz @Emilio Cobos Álvarez Please let me know if I got anything wrong or you want to add/modify anything to the list there, happy to update it.

I have definitely already done some work for prototyping the solution described in the issue. I was mostly waiting for a response so that I didn't post a PR that was just noise if it wasn't considered an acceptable solution. If it's acceptable to have the "one at a time solution" and that is blessed by @Emilio Cobos Álvarez, I'm happy to submit a PR in the near future. For the Clang side of things, I joined the Discord server and I'll post a question there just for some information gathering. They seem pretty friendly which is nice. :)
I made the mistake of asking in the LLVM chat #beginners channel and got not response so I'll try again after I've actually submitted the patch to bindgen in #clang.
I have had good luck asking on the discourse forum https://discourse.llvm.org/, I think more devs check that than discord

Oh cool, thanks! I'll try that too, but also realized Aaron Ballman was in the Discord and he responded when I pinged him directly about the issue. He also seems to have office hours semi regularly so I may be able to go to one of those and get some help with this.

I have two initial PRs for this work. One is the workaround, another is an example using it. Comments welcome. :)

https://github.com/rust-lang/rust-bindgen/compare/main...jbaublitz:rust-bindgen:issue-rust-bindgen-753
https://github.com/stratis-storage/libcryptsetup-rs/compare/master...jbaublitz:libcryptsetup-rs:issue-rust-bindgen-753
@Miguel Ojeda Particularly interested in your feedback for this before I submit it upstream.
You can add .truncate(true) to OpenOptions rather than deleting it first (also avoids an unlikely TOCTOU race)

Guard saves a nesting level at the end of parse_macro

    match parser.macro_definition(&cexpr_tokens) {
        Ok((_, (id, val))) => Some((id.into(), val)),
        _ if ctx.options().clang_macro_fallback => parse_macro_clang_fallback(ctx, cursor),
        _ => None
    }
Overall this looks pretty awesome :) Just open a PR, it's easier to add comments
Nice workaround! Do you have some info on how much slower it was to parse bindings_helper.h? Does libclang have some optimizations inside to not reparse the include file again for each query?

That is a nice user side diff @John Baublitz (i.e. removing all those lines in the header :)

What is the performance like? Does it feel too slow?

I just tried this patch on my machine and it's already running for more than 15minutes, I added a print statement in the parse_macro_clang_fallback and it looks like it does about 2-3 macros per second, so it seems there is no caching at least with my version of libclang. Normal bindgen takes only 1.9s for the same header, so this seems slow for general use.

Some time ago I did some experimentation with clang_reparseTranslationUnit and CXUnsavedFile which is API for the code highlighting in the editor. I was hoping this API will not reparse the whole header again, only the part which is supplied in the unsaved_files. However I was constantly getting SEGFAULTs and could not figure out what I was doing wrong, in the end I gave up. Maybe if you could get it to work it would give a boost in performance for the similar solution.
Did you try with Clang-Repl / libclangInterpreter?
No, but it maybe a good fit as well for this task
Is libclanginterpreter a drop in replacement? Is it just available via libclanginterpreter.so? I don't see that on my system, but I'm open to finding and testing it.

Trevor Gross said:

Guard saves a nesting level at the end of parse_macro

    match parser.macro_definition(&cexpr_tokens) {
        Ok((_, (id, val))) => Some((id.into(), val)),
        _ if ctx.options().clang_macro_fallback => parse_macro_clang_fallback(ctx, cursor),
        _ => None
    }

Okay I'll make these edits!

Miguel Ojeda said:

That is a nice user side diff John Baublitz (i.e. removing all those lines in the header :)

What is the performance like? Does it feel too slow?

It definitely feels slower, but it's hard to tell given how small the header I'm testing it on is. I could test it on Rust for Linux, but it looks like @Vadzim Dambrouski already did that.
It took 35 minutes to process the bindings_helper.h. I heard Linus Torvalds compiles his whole kernel in just 20 seconds (on threadripper) so I think we should try to keep it under 20 if possible.

@John Baublitz If you need some header file for testing, I extracted all defines from bindings_helper.h into a single file. It will parse a bit faster than real bindings_helper.h, because there are no structs/functions/etc, and you will loose some of the constants which rely on sizeof or enum variants. But it's still good for testing and you don't have to setup rust-for-linux every time you want to check something.

https://gist.github.com/pftbest/d333025cdd7e26e2aa766b403645cadf

Vadzim Dambrouski said:

John Baublitz If you need some header file for testing, I extracted all defines from bindings_helper.h into a single file. It will parse a bit faster than real bindings_helper.h, because there are no structs/functions/etc, and you will loose some of the constants which rely on sizeof or enum variants. But it's still good for testing and you don't have to setup rust-for-linux every time you want to check something.

https://gist.github.com/pftbest/d333025cdd7e26e2aa766b403645cadf

Thanks so much! This is super helpful.
I don't think Fedora provides anything for libclanginterpreter.so so I'm trying to build it from source.

John Baublitz said:

Is libclanginterpreter a drop in replacement? Is it just available via libclanginterpreter.so? I don't see that on my system, but I'm open to finding and testing it.

It is the library that clang-repl uses (which was inspired by Cling, i.e. the interpreter), and they have classes like PartialTranslationUnit and IncrementalParser, but I haven't tried them. I am also not sure how stable the API is supposed to be (i.e. we may need to anyway expose a stable API, and thus in that case we are back to square one because at that point we could do anything in LLVM because we would need to wait for a new release and so on).

In my local build I got it as a static library, but I would imagine BUILD_SHARED_LIBS would give you the shared one if you want that.

hmmm yeah that could be a problem if the API isn't stable. I'm currently trying to test alternate ways of parsing the files that allows caching of the header file, but I'm a little bit unsure of what the UnsavedFile parameter does in the larger context of the TranslationUnit

@John Baublitz I asked AI for the example usage, maybe it will help a little, not sure if it's correct though.

https://gist.github.com/pftbest/1cfcd41faa376f971f2ee4f5f66f5c78

If we imagine that example.c is your generated source which includes some target header and a first macro, you can use unsaved files to replace the whole contents of example.c with include header and a second macro. Supposedly it should not parse the header again in this scenario.
@Vadzim Dambrouski Offtopic: Only out of curiosity, what AI did you ask?
gpt-4-turbo via API
Thanks for the tip @Vadzim Dambrouski! I just implemented some changes that look like they speed up compilation significantly. Going to test on the header you provided sometime tonight.

./target/debug/bindgen --clang-macro-fallback $HOME/testheader.h  1.02s user 0.08s system 97% cpu 1.117 total
./target/debug/bindgen $HOME/testheader.h  1.03s user 0.08s system 97% cpu 1.133 total
I think it's working! Please feel free to test yourselves too.
Please share a link to new commit
https://github.com/rust-lang/rust-bindgen/compare/main...jbaublitz:rust-bindgen:issue-rust-bindgen-753
Also I forgot to mention last time, don't rely on $OUT_DIR variable, it's not set when running from command line. If it's missing your code silently ignores this error and don't parse any macros
@John Baublitz Please check the time once again, I think you had this problem with OUT_DIR so the code did not run.
For me it's taking much longer than one second, something is not right.

Vadzim Dambrouski said:

Also I forgot to mention last time, don't rely on $OUT_DIR variable, it's not set when running from command line. If it's missing your code silently ignores this error and don't parse any macros

Ah this is a good point. Where would recommend handling this? /tmp?
You could try env!("OUT_DIR") which stores it at compile time
Wait nevermind don't do that, I'm reading the wrong thing
You might be able to copy the logic for picking an output file with the option that generates inline wrappers

Vadzim Dambrouski said:

John Baublitz If you need some header file for testing, I extracted all defines from bindings_helper.h into a single file. It will parse a bit faster than real bindings_helper.h, because there are no structs/functions/etc, and you will loose some of the constants which rely on sizeof or enum variants. But it's still good for testing and you don't have to setup rust-for-linux every time you want to check something.

https://gist.github.com/pftbest/d333025cdd7e26e2aa766b403645cadf

One thing I noticed about this header file is it doesn't have the necessary includes to actually expand any of the macros. How do you handle that in your tests? Do you include this header file after the necessary Linux headers in a separate file? If so, can you provide the names of the ones I need for this?

Trevor Gross said:

You might be able to copy the logic for picking an output file with the option that generates inline wrappers

Can you point me to where this is in the source code?

@John Baublitz As I said before this file only contains defines, the typedefs and structs are missing. You can't provide them with any system header. If you want to expand all of the macros from that file you need to use a full "bindings_helper.h" from Rust-For-Linux instead
This one https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/blob/rust-next/rust/bindings/bindings_helper.h

Vadzim Dambrouski said:

John Baublitz As I said before this file only contains defines, the typedefs and structs are missing. You can't provide them with any system header. If you want to expand all of the macros from that file you need to use a full "bindings_helper.h" from Rust-For-Linux instead

I understand that it only contains the defines, but it also contains defines that contain function-like macros in the body. To appropriately test the expansion of those I need the definition for those function-like macros. I don't think we'll get a really accurate view of how long it takes for defines alone if it doesn't contain enough information to expand all of the defines in the file. You mentioned that it doesn't have structs and typedefs. I don't think either of these are necessary for expanding all of the defines, we would just need to definitions of the function-like macros in addition to all of defines that use them in the body to fully test this. If that's better suited to "bindings_helper.h", I can try that.

@John Baublitz It has all of the function macros there, please check again. only types are missing. It's just they are not in original order as gcc reports it that way. But the order doesn't matter for function like macro, as they can be defined in any order before the first use.
Which macro did you see as missing ?

Hm it looks like they are all there. I'm trying to track down why the expansions seem to be working in my test for the library I'm developing these changes against, but not for the CLI invocation on the header your provided with all of the #defines. I can see how order doesn't matter in this case and the defines are definitely there, so I'll try debugging a bit more and see if I can resolve the issue. Thanks!
Also I saw a couple of macros that are really missing, and from the original bindings_helper.h as well. This can definitely happen, but most of them should be there
Okay, good to know. The ones I'm specifically using as a kind of quick "did this work" indication are GENMASK() and _IO(). If those don't get expanded, it seems that it's not working.
And both of those are definitely present from the search I did.
GENMASK should work 100%. I made a list of all the failed ones, you may check it here https://gist.github.com/pftbest/d948e310502f9553cecf56c141631ab6
By failed I mean they are not evaluated to a constant value by gcc

@Vadzim Dambrouski @Miguel Ojeda I have some more ideas to work on next week. I talked with the Clang folks and it seems that a precompiled header could significantly speed up bindgen.

My preliminary results with some manual steps for precompiling the header:

./target/debug/bindgen --clang-macro-fallback $HOME/testheader.h  4.79s user 1.68s system 48% cpu 13.377 total

I also verified that it isn't silently failing on the macros that need to be expanded with the fallback and they seem to be generating correctly.
https://github.com/rust-lang/rust-bindgen/pull/2779 I've moved the candidate code to Github in a PR. Feel free to comment there.
Left some comments :) 3.5s sounds quite usable! Thanks a lot John!
Okay sounds good! I'll take a look at the comments as soon as I have time.
@Miguel Ojeda Now that the bindgen PR is merged, should we request a release?
It would not hurt. We want a few more things from bindgen, but perhaps it is a good time to try an update anyway.
If you want to point me to other things I could help with in bindgen, I'm willing to work on them!

That is great, thanks!

https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/issues/353
https://github.com/rust-lang/rust-bindgen/labels/rust-for-linux (our label there)

In particular, https://github.com/rust-lang/rust-bindgen/issues/2674 and https://github.com/rust-lang/rust-bindgen/issues/2646 are a couple feature additions about generating Rust code (so hopefully fun :).
Okay, I'll take a look!
@Miguel Ojeda Just an FYI that someone found two bugs in my original macro work so I'm working on fixing it here. https://github.com/rust-lang/rust-bindgen/pull/2823
Thanks John! I was following the progress from my email :) I have linked it to our RFL issue now.
